content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I need both standard and unique takes on time loop movies

**Score:** 26
**Body:** (Please specify which category your suggestion belongs to. 😭)

I have a theme for every month of this year. January is time loop movies. I want to take this chance to study the subgenre. Can y'all recommend me a time loop starter pack, and then time loop movies that stretch and twist the potential of the genre?

* I have other posts with time loop asks saved, but I made this one so y'all could specify which ones are basic and which ones are more interesting takes on it. Also, I'd like to recommend Source Code with Jake Gyllenhaal. It has a lot of heart for a sci-fi film.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Groundhog Day and Edge of Tomorrow are your bread and butter starters. For the weird stuff, check out Triangle (2009) - that one will mess with your head in the best way. Predestination also does some wild things with the concept but leans more into sci-fi territory
2. Palm Springs and Happy Death Day
  - Palm Springs is so funny and it is definitely a unique take on the time loop movie
3. Beyond the Infinite Two Minutes (2020) - *A cafe owner discovers that the TV in his cafe suddenly shows images from the future, but only two minutes into the future.*
4. Time Crimes
5. *”About Time”* (2013)

*”Arrival”* (2016) - Advanced concepts

*”Looper”* 2012)

*”The Time Traveler’s Wife”* (2009)

*”The Edge Of Tomorrow”* (2014)
6. A more obscure one is the indie film The Endless. It's about time loops but it's a really fresh take on the whole concept.
7. Season 1 of Russian Doll.  I know, not a movie.  But it is  a unique take.
8. Primer
9. 12 monkeys

Looper 

Timecop

The Source Code

Lola Rennt

## 2. I’m looking for movies where people fall in love love each other throughout the entire movie stay in love get a happy ending all of that but things are just completely fucked outside of that.

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I’m looking for two people that find love in like the worst of circumstances, and just win through the power of love. I mean, it can be anything from like alien invasion to end of the world to zombie to just in jail together but at no point do they waiver on each other even with the world being a complete wreck and mess and somehow there’s a happy ending. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Natural Born Killers

Bonnie and Clyde

Big Fat Greek Wedding

Palm Springs
2. True romance
  - Came here to say this
3. Wild at Heart 

“This whole world is wild at heart and we’re on top!”
4. The Last of the Mohicans
5. Alfred Hitchcock's Rebecca

Raising Arizona

Drive away dolls
6. Delicatessen (1991) fits this perfectly.  
How fucked the world is will amaze you.  
The happy ending will amaze you.

It’s literally one of my favorite film endings of all time.

Watch with english subtitles and French language is superior to the english language dub.
7. The Money Pit
8. Raising Arizona (1987) with Holly Hunter and Nicholas Cage
9. Warm Bodies

## 3. Psychological Thrillers

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Quick my boyfriend and I want to watch a psychological thriller that will absolutely change our minds— similar to Fight Club.

The options we have are:

\- Unsane (2018

\- The machinist 

\- Parasite 

We’re also open to other options 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Try Ex-Machina
2. *Memento*, *The Game*, and *Insomnia* probably fit the bill.

Edit: Ah my bad, you were asking among those choices… *Parasite* is a social commentary more than a mind-bender.
3. Parasite, without a doubt
4. Angel Heart
  - Underrated flick.
5. Watch “Parasite.” The other choices are good films too. But “Parasite” is a great film.
6. Parasite

Unsane was meh.
7. parasite
8. Felon with Val Kilmer
9. Watch the french film la mustache - it’s about a man who shaves his mustache off, but no one remembers he ever had one, so he starts to go insane. Very underrated, very french, very solid psych thriller

## 4. Movie to watch while grieving a father, with some father son content.

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I don’t know exactly what kind of content I’m after. Positive of course. Just looking for movies with father/son content to watch while grieving the death of my father.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sorry for your loss man. Big Fish always hits different when dealing with father stuff - it's about understanding your dad through stories and memory. The Pursuit of Happyness is another solid choice if you want something uplifting about the father-son bond
2. About Time (2013). The father/son relationship is big in this movie.
  - Came here to say this. It’s a really beautiful part of a beautiful film.
  - I ugly cry every time. Such a beautiful Beautiful film
3. Field of Dreams might be too heavy. How about father figures in Secondhand Lions; a light, easy movie.

Sorry for your loss, I miss my dad.
4. The Judge with Robert Downey Jr and Robert Duval
5. Life as a House
6. The Road -based on the Cormac McCarthy book
7. Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
8. Field of Dreams

## 5. Thrillers with the following

**Score:** 5
**Body:** These are just some of the things I love to see in thriller movies. Doesn’t have to include them but would be nice to have:

Plot Twist

Realistic Story (No Sci Fi or Fantasy)

Modern. Maybe in the past 25-30 years

Here are some of my favorite thrillers so you kind of have an idea of what I am looking for

1. The Prestige

2. Prisoners

3. Se7en

4. Zodiac

5. Parasite

6. Sinners

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. L.A. Confidential (1997)
2. The Game
3. The Insider, Training Day, Enemy of the State, Breakdown, Collateral
4. Narc
5. Memento

Primal Fear
6. You’re Next
7. Start with The Game if you haven’t yet seen

No Country for Old Men

Sicario
8. Silence of the Lambs.  

Also The Vanishing—the Dutch original, the Hollywood remake stinks.  

No Way Out w/ Kevin Costner.  

And it’s a bit older than your parameters, but still “modern”:  The Parallax View, w/ Warren Beatty.
9. No sci fi or fantasy but cloning machines and vampires are 'A' okay. Hahaha had a good laugh, great list OP some of my faves as well 

If you love Prestige have you seen the Illusionist? Very good as well.
10. Gone Girl (2014) 

The Gift (2015)

The Girl With The Dragon Tattoo (2009 Swedish original, that is)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Which movie was this for you?

**Score:** 3513

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved Batman & Robin when I was younger.  I still do, but now in a "so bad it's good" way.
  - I remember when I was 9, I begged my older sister to take me to see Batman and Robin in theaters. 

In the movie, there's a moment when Batman and Robin click their heels together, and ice skates pop out of their boots... and I distinctly remember looking over at my sister and feeling really embarrassed that I drug her to this movie. 

I was 9.
2. xXx with Vin Diesel. My brothers and I were obsessed with that movie when we were kids. Biggest cheese fest of a movie I’ve seen to this day though now
  - That movie got me into rammstein and ultimately into the industrial scene. I’ll always be grateful for that alone.
3. Nostalgia usually pushes me through The Power Rangers Movie. Still love it. Also Airborne.
4. Blank Check
  - "Blank Check" is wild considering it had a romance subplot with a grown woman and a child.
5. Quest For Camelot circa 1998 Warner Bros animation - hot garbage that I was OBSESSED with as a 6yo 😂
6. Mario brothers movie may be garbage but it’s my favorite mario movie. Just a completely different beast than what anything else dares to try and it almost destroyed lives
  - Bob Hoskins and John Leguizamo, let's ride! (our jump boots)

## 2. Who here has seen Eternity? Is it sticking with anyone else?

**Score:** 68
**Body:** This movie has been like gum for me. It's just been stuck to me for the last week. Every time I start to space out, I'm going back to it. 

At first I thought I thought it was Elizabeth Olsen, cause it's Lizzie. But it's deeper than just one person, or Miles and Callum - who I also happen to like. Then I thought it was the plot. But, the destination was - overall - predictable. Though, the journey was the part that had unexpected turns. 

I think it was just that perfect mixture of plot, and editing, and acting that pushed it to a higher degree or Romance, or Romantic Comedy, than it otherwise would have been. Don't get me wrong, its no *Some Like it Hot* or *When Harry Met Sally,* but it may be on that next tier. Memorable, but not an all-time classic. 

I think what it did to stick was to hit slowly and unassuming. Let's face it, when you turn on a Romcom - or most movies - a part of your brain shuts down or goes into neutral, and you are able to simply watch and enjoy. That's where *Eternity* hits you. 

It forces your "neutral" brain to kick back in. Suddenly, you feel nostalgic, yet hopeful. There's sadness and joy. You contemplate the mistakes and people of your past. Mainly, your jealous of the character's yet root for them as they arrive at their joyous resolutions. 

Thoughts from the group?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Biggest surprise of the year, felt like something straight out of the 90s. Delightful, original, and a fun watch
  - Exactly! Just like those three little words, everyone longs to hear *You've got Mail!*

Honestly, *We Live in Time*, underwhelming. *Eternity*, surprising and very rewatchable!
  - This sold me. I haven't watched it yet and I been thinking about checking it out. Hearing that it's like the 90s makes me feel happy and can't wait to watch it.
2. I'm excited to be able to stream it at home. It stuck with me for a while after leaving the theater, but I want to sit with it more.


I thought the story structure was excellent and it had been a while since I'd seen a high-concept rom com work this well.


My favorite movie of the century so far is About Time (2013), and while it isn't the best film of the century, it just has so much to say about life and love.


I think that's Eternity for me.
3. It's a crime that this movie got scant exposure. Meanwhile, the marketing for *Ella McKay* felt like a case of post-Thanksgiving herpes.
  - It's weird to me to read this, because I've seen a little bit about this movie but I have absolutely no idea who or what Ella McKay is.

I have, however, seen a lot about Song Sung Blue and I'm still confused about why there's a movie about a Neil Diamond cover band which is very serious and not a Farrelly Brothers comedy starring Will Ferrell.
    - I've had this discussion before, and it baffles me how some people managed to completely avoid the McKay marketing blitz. Starting the last weekend of November, I couldn't escape the ads, either on TV or social media. It's clear to me now that this was definitely strategic targeting, as I'm in the 30+ demographic, and a fair share of the commercials were boasting the Simpsons connection. No joke, Disney even released an animated short of Marge and Lisa leaving the theater in utter awe of the movie. Admittedly, I was close to getting hooked, but my partner was the deciding factor in avoiding it. As the ad exposure aggressively amped up around the week of release, I pretty much figured that this was gonna be a steaming pile of horseshit.

In response to your original statement, the movie is a pseudo-romantic political dramedy that aims to recapture that feel-good energy of 90s rom-coms. To no surprise, the movie fails on almost every conceivable level. Again, zero shock here, given that this was an 85 year-old auteur's big comeback after a 15 year hiatus.
4. Thought it might be a little shallow due to the concept, but it does more with the material than I expected and there's some genuinely heart tugging moments. I'm not a man of faith in any regard, but seeing the couple reunite after death definitely hit me with feelings about my grandparents that caught me off guard.

Overall, a pretty good film, performances were great, it's also quite a funny film at times, they really make use of the concept. If I would criticise anything about the film it would be to do with the film's ending so spoilers: >! I thought the character of Luke could have been better explored and resolved. At the end he just sort of steps aside without much insight into what he exists for besides making Joan happy. Everyone else gets to have their arc of growth except him. !<
5. In fact, I watched it without any expectations and without getting my hopes up about what I might find, but it ended up being a pleasant surprise 👌🏻 I think it's a romantic comedy that actually surprises you with a touch of nostalgia and a bit of reflection on the life we ​​live and the life we ​​could have lived, which is not surprising when you touch on these kinds of topics like death, the choices we make and the repercussions they have on our future life.
6. I watched because of Elizabeth Olsen and Miles Teller. Enjoyed it. Was really bland. And to imagine to live again after death, and feel pain, that made me depressed.

## 3. Sad Eastwood is Done

**Score:** 22
**Body:** I thought Juror no 2 was great. And a solid end to an incredible career. 

Is Eastwood a top 10 American director all time?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He's a great director
2. Unforgiven is one of the best films of all time, and nobody but Clint could have made it. In general, I think in his filmography, while sometime of mixed quality, he always finds some aspect of the story that connects with him personally and amplifies it. In Unforgiven for example he takes a great script with themes about the cyclical nature of violence and how myth turns bad people or ideas into legends, and connects it with his own career of playing violent, pulpy anti-heroes, linking the mythology of the West in the past with the medium of film itself.
3. Not sure about Top 10, but definitely Top 20.
4. I think Clint Eastwood was a pretty incredible actor, and his movies were fantastic

I would likely list them in my top 10, but I know a lot of people won’t for one reason or the other
5. Movies like midnight in the garden of good evil is great

Invicus is an outstanding movie

Mystic river and million dollar baby are both great

Of course you have the unforgiven

An older films like the outlaw Josie Wales is just a great film
6. That's sad and it's gonna be a very sad day when he passes, but he's easily in the top 10, if not the top 5.
7. I'm the only person I know who loved Cry Macho
8. Has he ever confirmed he's retired? Everything I've heard/seen was just people saying Juror No. 2 could be his last movie because of his age, but if he was directing at 93 there's no reason he couldn't be directing at 95-96.
9. From a popularity, commercial successes or accolade success standpoint… I would say no.
  - I don't think commercial success should be a factor; many truly great filmmakers have never had huge commercial films. As for accolade successes, Eastwood does have four Oscars which is far more than most.

## 4. Favorite cases where a "douchey" character is relatively harmless, and not a full-blown villain?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Im rewatching Forgetting Sarah Marshall and Russell Brand's character is a pretentious douche but he also genuinely tries to be friends with Peter (Jason Segel)

For TV you could say Howard from BCS, initially comes off as a dick yet he is pretty morally upstanding overall.

Was curious if you guys have any characters who come off as dickheads, but ultimately aren't really bad at the core? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Iceman in Top Gun is a total douche, but he’s a professional fighter pilot who doesn’t approve of taking unnecessary risks.
2. Ellis from Diehard.


When he goes in to negotiate with Hans Gruber, even though he's a coke addled scumbag who is trying to bang Holly- he doesn't give up the info that Holly is John's wife. Even when faced with his own death in his final moments.
3. Stifler. 

Doakes in Dexter.
4. Bill Paxton (the used car salesman) in True Lies 
5. Ed Rooney. He’s almost a sympathetic character all these years later. 
  - I used to think this until I realized his greater aim is to get Ferris in trouble, not for him to be a better student. His interest in Ferris is to catch him and get him in trouble to show to the rest of the students that no one is above the “law”. He wasn’t an educator interested in Ferris’s future.
6. Harry Ellis from Die Hard…”Hans, bubby, I’m your white knight.”
7. Benny from The Mummy (1999) a scumbag comic villain
8. Kent in REAL GENIUS? Not a villain, but rather what happens to people when they get too sexually frustrated. (At least that's how Chris Knight describes him.)
9. Hansel. So hot right now.

## 5. Top 5 Adam Sandler Movies?

**Score:** 11
**Body:** Wondering everybody’s top five Adam Sandler movies?  I’ve regrown a lot of love for Sandler over the last little while, and want to show some appreciation, hear some other takes, and potentially get some recommendations for great films of his I haven’t yet indulged in.  I’ll start with mine.

1. Uncut Gems

I know a lot of people hate this movie due to the anxiety inducing nature and lack of likeable characters, but that is right up my alley, and I think it is Adam Sandler’s Greatest performance ever.  He just effortlessly becomes the manipulative, narcissistic gambling addict in over his head.  I eat it up.  Love it.

2. The Wedding Singer

This is the Sandler Comedy where he is the most subdued, but still classic Sandler.  It has the most Heart, in my opinion.  Fantastic romantic comedy, hits my hopeless romantic heart like a truck.  I cry every time during the “I Want To Grow Old With You” song.  Sandler and Barrymore have unbelievable chemistry.

3. Punch Drunk Love

Is Sandler making a PTA movie or is PTA making an Adam Sandler movie?  Don’t know, don’t care.  It’s awesomely weird, anxiety inducing and heartwarming.  PSH is fantastic.  And an all time great dramatic performance from Sandler, proving he is a great actor, not just the funny loud guy from SNL.

4. Big Daddy

Another heartwarming classic Sandler comedy.  Any movie where “I wipe my own ass!” Can bring you to tears is a winner in my book.

5. Happy Gilmore

Straight up, classic Adam Sandler comedy.  So many quotable lines, so many memorable characters.  One of the greatest golf movies.  Ben Stiller cameo as the abusive nursing home worker is hilarious.  This just beat Waterboy by a hair for me.

Let me know your rankings, and your opinions on his movies!   Give me recommendations, and gush about my picks along with me.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great list. Hard to argue with Uncut Gems at #1 — that performance was unreal. Punch Drunk Love is also such a special pick, still feels underrated. My top five would overlap a lot, but I’d probably sneak The Waterboy or Click in there for nostalgia and heart. Sandler really shines when he mixes comedy with real emotion.
2. "50 First Dates" not making the cut? Sandler's charm peeling off the layers of Drew Barrymore's amnesia each day was pure gold. But solid choices otherwise, can't really argue with Big Daddy's self-wiping victory.
3. The meyerowitz stories would absolutely be on there for me
4. Little Nicky 'Popeyes chicken, is fackin awesome' 


The Waterboy 'Medulla Oblongata'


The Wedding Singer 'I have a microphone, and you don't, SO YOU WILL LISTEN TO EVERY DAMN WORD I HAVE TO SAY!'


Spanglish



Airheads


I think Spanglish is a great film. Written and directed by James L Brooks, if you seen his other films you will know.
5. With Uncut being your top pick that’s all I needed to see. Get this guy a drink on me  🍹
6. Happy Gilmore

Reign Over Me

Hustle

Billy Madison

Uncut Gems
  - Reign Over Me was fantastic but add it to the list of movies I will never watch again.  Straight gut punch
7. He was the heart and soul of "Jay Kelly," a movie i didn't love. Even though his was a supporting role, he really made the movie.

Also "Uncut Gems" and "The Meyerowitz Stories," most definitely.
8. In no particular order

Big daddy
Billy Madison
Hustle
Grown ups
Blended
9. Click hits my heart like a truck

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Martin Scorsese recent films border on underated

**Score:** 168
**Body:** I feel that Scorsese’s fame is almost working against him. While his films are typically among the biggest releases of their respective years, The Irishman, Silence, and Killers of the Flower Moon all seem to fall out of film discourse rather quickly. In the case of the first two especially, I would even say they are bordering on underrated, largely because people constantly compare them to his very best work. Killers here.  being the only one I saw get a lot of praise across that entire year it came out. 

If these three films had somehow been made by an up and coming director, I think he would instantly be regarded as one of the most exciting names in Hollywood, and these films would be discussed constantly. Of the three, only Killers of the Flower Moon feels as though it has received something close to the recognition it deserves. Silence has become a semi recognised classic among certain audiences, but The Irishman in particular feels strangely under praised. Many people get overly caught up on the de aging effects, which do not meaningfully affect the story if you look past them, and this has overshadowed discussion of the film itself. It is also surprisingly difficult to find discourse about it today, despite it being one of the most grounded and realistic mob films ever made with what I consider to be an incredible 3rd act and ending 

I suspect many viewers were expecting another adrenaline filled Casino style film. Silence is easier to understand in this regard, as its subject matter is not especially appealing to mass audiences and it was never going to have broad commercial appeal. More generally, I think there is a strong desire to focus on younger directors, which means that what Scorsese is doing now is often under discussed or taken for granted. The praise becomes muted because the reaction is "Its Scorsese what do you expect!" 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Silence is an absolute masterpiece. Honestly, I was stunned by Killers of the Flower Moon, and would say that Scorcese moved into his last phase of filmmaking like e.g. Akira Kurosawa or Ingmar Bergman!
2. I totally agree.

The way he shot the violence in The Irishman and Flower Moon felt different. Unceremonious, ugly, flat. Unsatisfying. The spoils the characters get from their evil aren't as convincing, aren't as seductive. Sheeran and Burkhart are dullards with no charisma, no drive,  no agency. 

Marty's films have always had a moral clarity to them but he doesn't really depict sin in that ironic seductive way anymore. It's just kind of... sad. People doing bad things for the vague idea of money. They lose everything that matters to them and they have a pronounced sense of having wasted their lives for no good reason. 

I so deeply admire that he's still evolving as an artist, that he's finding new ways of looking at his lifelong concerns and exploring different angles. So many filmmakers settle into routine and stop evolving when they reach a certain age but he has never lost his curiosity.
  - I agree with you completely, but he had already experimented with this feeling of misery instead of "glorification" in Taxi Driver. That shootout at the end is one of the most violent things I've ever seen, and it's not because of blood or gore or whatever. It just feels wrong.

He just seems to have circled back to it in terms of how he portrays violence.
  - I would like to try and give a somewhat different perspective here. I agree with everything you said about no longer getting caught up with the “allure” of sin, but I think it’s not just the “vague idea of money” motivating Sheeran and Burkhart. I believe it is, to put it one way, a lack of imagination. That is to say, they are both war veterans who’ve seen the worst of the world and they go through life unable to imagine a life that isn’t like crabs in a barrel fighting for their life. If there’s an evil person in the world asking you to do evil things for them, you just do it. That’s the way of the world. If they one day ask you to kill somebody that you actually began to love, you do it. The world is their domain. It is safer under the dragon’s wing. One telling line in killers of the flower moon is when deniro is talking about the miracles in the bible and says “you know miracles don’t happen anymore.” These characters are faithless, but sympathetic. They feel shame, but they don’t feel sorry. 

That is what sin is. Not a will to do evil but having the lack of faith that doing good rather than evil means anything. To a person who has seen the worst in the world, this isn’t always an easy thing to convince them of and it’s something that the rest of us aren’t usually forced to contend with. Burkhart says “sometimes in life you do what you don’t want to do and you don’t do what you do want to do” which is almost directly from St. Paul

Frank says he is sorry, but he doesn’t feel sorry. Perhaps what he means is that he is sorry for the way that the world is more than he is sorry for who he is or what he did. Scorsese talks about the sacred feeling in a hospital at night. It is this combination of nighttime, sickness and total stillness (in the still of the night 🎶🎵) that winds up feeling like an open-ended question. I don’t think it’s simply that Frank is experiencing pain and loneliness for his sin. He is experiencing the pain of never having imagined for himself anything besides this bleak world he took part in. But that pain gives way to the sacred eventually, as things start to become still and the chaos of the world has fallen away (even if the suffering hasn’t yet). The nightmare that was his life is finally coming to an end. There’s still some light there in spite of it all, the door hasn’t closed fully on him
3. The last hour of The Irishman is some of the best cinema Scorsese has ever directed. Audience expectations were out of line, Netflix promoted it strangely, the CGI looks kind of bad, but none of that matters when you watch the last hour and everything pays off. Powerful, spiritual, intense, it's peak Marty. Unfortunately many people don't make it there as that run time is much too much for most streaming audiences.
  - Man, when Deniro is hugging Pacino in the car knowing he's gonna kill him. Might be the saddest thing Scorsese's ever filmed.
4. Yeah, I remember as credits rolled for “Killers of the Flower Moon” and I saw Marty’s cameo I had a sudden realization that, 

“holy shit, its been 10 years since The Wolf of Wall Street that Im seeing a Scorsese film in theaters.”

We are still lucky that a director of his stature is still making great movies that you can go and watch in the theaters. I don’t know how many more we’re gonna get but, I’ll be there for every single one.
5. The Irishman is underpraised? It was widely considered an instant classic when it came out and nominated for 10 Academy Awards. On pretty much every major publications' top movies of the 21st century list.

It was an absolute phenomenon when it came out. Only reason I can think that you may not remember this is because COVID happened a couple months after its release.
6. I love this late period Marty as much as his previous works. Each film feels like a man looking back and deciding he has a lot to say about his life’s work and putting that into his art. 

The Irishman of course about his gangster films and violent, broken men they depict. Silence in conversation with his religious films and spiritually. And then Killers of the Flower Moon as an American Epic looking into the dark hearts of men and how it shaped America today. 

These movies also feel unburdened by studio or box office concerns so yes they’re long but I’ll take every extra minute we can get from this master.
7. All three are gorgeous movies.     He is taking very big swings and working on huge canvases, and there will always be plenty of small things to find fault with.

Killers of the Flower Moon is perhaps my favorite of the three.   It felt like he was taking everything he learned from his own work, and everything he learned from film he loves and applying it all in his own way.   The composition of the shots, the production design (if you can track down the NY Times piece featuring the production designer, it's jaw-dropping) and his letting the film breathe so the Osage had many moments to feature their ceremonies and voices.     It's a film that you can go back to again and again.    

(I particularly love the first ten minutes of the film, the "old time film reel," the transition into the train, the bustling scene at the train station, the car ride through the endless landscape, all telescoping down to the embrace between De Niro and DiCaprio with the camera swirling around them.  Breathtaking).

The de-aging in The Irishman really keeps a lot of people from enjoying the film, and the earliest scenes look clumsy.  (Worst moments are the "20-year old De Niro" and the curb-stomping scene).   But those moments aside, it's all his gangster pictures played in a minor key.    The middle section with Pacino is full of bravura moments and fun storytelling, and I frequently go back just to watch the Latin Casino sequence.    Pacino and Pesci get that one fantastic scene together.

And Silence is just rapturous and beautiful.    I have a copy of it and will go back every few years to admire it.

(You didn't mention the other late-career work, Wolf of Wall Street.  That one does get a lot of attention but again, it is a three-hour mega-film.   Scorsese is not taking is easy or making it simple -- he is reaching as far as he can with the limited time he has left)

## 2. Kill Bill's ending shows the refutation of Bill's Superman/Clark Kent speech

**Score:** 6
**Body:** The speech from Bill is at it's most direct laying out how Beatrix Kiddo couldn't live a normal life because it would just be a cover for her true nature as a trained killer, in the same way that to him, Clark Kent is Superman's disguise and that his true self is this superior being who's fundamentally destined to be a Superhero. 

Bill's speech is all kinds of wrong, both in reference to Superman and in reference to Beatrix herself. Whilst through the film we do see her going on a killing spree of her own desire, we see just as much how she either avoids murdering people or simply isn't able to. Not to mention, whilst she did serve as an assassin for Bill, she was ready to give it all up for motherhood and a regular life. It's not like Superman's double life, there would be no The Bride if not for the actions of Bill. 

Not to mention, Bill considers Clark Kent to be Superman's way of mocking the flaws of humanity and their mediocre qualities, and yet Beatrix herself took on this different identity because it was what she wanted for herself. There's no mockery of real people. Whilst the speech wasn't intended by Bill to be a total one to one with Beatrix, it's a speech made to not link up with her and shows you how shallow and cynical Bill's perspective on her is.

As for the ending specifically, whilst it's left relatively open and has it's own set of ambiguities, it does seem to suggest that she'll be able to live the life that Bill tried to take from her. Essentially, she's choosing to be Beatrix Kiddo and no longer ever has to be The Bride unless Nikki comes to her in 20 years.

It's refuting Bill's monologue because it's not only telling us that the trained killer side of her can be put to rest at least for now, but that a loving mother is what's fundamentally a part of her identity and especially from now on will BE her identity. It won't just simply be the disguise that Bill thinks she was trying to wear in changing her name and marrying someone. And it helps that in order to lead a normal life, she may not need to go around with a fake name or marry anyone to do it.

Notice that even when she thinks she's lost her child, she still does have a certain instinct towards kids. Like spanking that one teenage member of the Crazy 88 rather than killing him, showing a flash of uncomfortably at killing Gogo and giving her a chance to leave, and apologising to Nikki Green after killing her mother whilst letting her know that it wasn't her intention to do that in front of her. Plus, there's obviously her wanting to live for her daughter and live away from the assassin lifestyle which is why she ran away in the first place.

On a sidenote, Beatrix saying "Thank you" at the end could be towards two people in my mind. It could be towards Bill for keeping her daughter alive, giving her something to live for. Or it could be towards God or whoever was controlling her fate for letting her have a happy ending.

The biggest thing I noticed is that there was three deaths and rebirths. Each of these deaths were shedding aspects of Beatrix’s  character, and each of these defined Beatrix’s character actions thereafter. 
The first was the death of the bride, by being shot by Bill, which allowed the rebirth of the assassin. She then goes on a killing spree. 

The second death, her burial, is the death of the assassin and is reborn as Beatrix. This is the point Elle gives us the name reveal. She doesn’t kill anyone going forward until after her third death.

The third death is of Beatrix, by being shot by her daughter, and she is reborn, and renamed by her daughter, as Mommy. This is also what the Superman monologue is about. Bill’s abusive grooming her into who he thinks she should be, and ultimately her attempts at rejecting that. In the end her daughter is the one who truly changes her.

## 3. Thoughts on Rosemead (2025)?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** Recently watched this at a theatre. I really wanted to like it, but in the end I had mixed feelings on it.

A lot of people use "one-dimensional" to refer to characters who you care nothing for in genre schlock (e.g. heroes in generic action movies, leads in romantic slop). But I think it is also a label applicable to characters whose only purpose  seems to be to make the audience cry.

Unfortunately, I think the main characters in this melodrama fit this label perfectly. The mother is defined as "having terminal cancer" and "having a mentally ill son". The son is defined solely as "mentally ill" and "having a mom with terminal cancer". You could not write a more cliched melodramatic set of main characters if you tried. Oh and I guess they both have the extra character trait of "is Chinese", which is something I'll get to in just a second.

For a melodrama based on real life, thought there would be some meaningful social commentary at least. But in the end, it seems the most interesting thing the writer could muster was "oh look at how much the Chinese stigmatize mental illness". As a Chinese American I definitely think this is true, but if you're going to take a real life tragedy like this and turn it into a goddamn *movie* you better have more insight beyond something this basic. They also briefly allude to the state's psychiatry system, but they never really go into that at all. 

I'm surprised at how many glowing reviews this has gotten. Lots of people I see praised this movie because it made them cry. I feel like this movie is the equivalent of restaurants who make their crappy food very spicy, and trick customers into thinking "if eating the food makes me feel pain then it has to be good". Sorry, but shit food with 3000 thai chillies spammed on top of it is still shit food. Similarly, bad writing with 3000 things that are supposed to make you feel bad is still bad.

This movie reminds me of another "prestige" melodrama I watched previously, called The Sunny Side of the Street (it's a movie from Hong Kong). Just like with this movie, the characters are not defined by anything beyond personal tragedies. Both movies feel transparently manipulative.

Thoughts?

## 4. Top 5 Adam Sandler Movies?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Wondering everybody’s top five Adam Sandler movies?  I’ve regrown a lot of love for Sandler over the last little while, and want to show some appreciation, hear some other takes, and potentially get some recommendations for great films of his I haven’t yet indulged in.  I’ll start with mine.

1. Uncut Gems

I know a lot of people hate this movie due to the anxiety inducing nature and lack of likeable characters, but that is right up my alley, and I think it is Adam Sandler’s Greatest performance ever.  He just effortlessly becomes the manipulative, narcissistic gambling addict in over his head.  I eat it up.  Love it.

2. The Wedding Singer

This is the Sandler Comedy where he is the most subdued, but still classic Sandler.  It has the most Heart, in my opinion.  Fantastic romantic comedy, hits my hopeless romantic heart like a truck.  I cry every time during the “I Want To Grow Old With You” song.  Sandler and Barrymore have unbelievable chemistry.

3. Punch Drunk Love

Is Sandler making a PTA movie or is PTA making an Adam Sandler movie?  Don’t know, don’t care.  It’s awesomely weird, anxiety inducing and heartwarming.  PSH is fantastic.  And an all time great dramatic performance from Sandler, proving he is a great actor, not just the funny loud guy from SNL.

4. Big Daddy

Another heartwarming classic Sandler comedy.  Any movie where “I wipe my own ass!” Can bring you to tears is a winner in my book.

5. Happy Gilmore

Straight up, classic Adam Sandler comedy.  So many quotable lines, so many memorable characters.  One of the greatest golf movies.  Ben Stiller cameo as the abusive nursing home worker is hilarious.  This just beat Waterboy by a hair for me.

Let me know your rankings, and your opinions on his movies!   Give me recommendations, and gush about my picks along with me.
